title: Job 4
subtitle: Eliphaz’s First Speech: The Innocent Don’t Perish

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — "Who That Was Innocent Ever Perished?"

heb.paragraph: "Who, being innocent (nāqî), has ever perished?" (v.7). Eliphaz states the retribution principle: the innocent do not suffer ultimately. This is not stupid theology — it is the mainstream position of Proverbs and Deuteronomy. Eliphaz’s error is not that the principle is always wrong but that it is always right.

panels.ctx: Eliphaz begins gently: "you have instructed many, you have strengthened feeble hands" (vv.3–4). He honours Job’s past before delivering his diagnosis. His argument: you taught others to endure; now endure yourself. If you are truly innocent, God will restore you. The unspoken implication: if you are not restored, you are not innocent.

cross.note: "The LORD does not let the righteous go hungry" — the proverbial basis for Eliphaz’s theology.
"I have never seen the righteous forsaken" — the experiential claim Eliphaz makes.

notes.note: Eliphaz opens with respect. He acknowledges Job’s past ministry to others. This is not a cruel man; it is a sincere one with a flawed framework.
"Should not your piety be your confidence?" The logic: if you’re truly righteous, have hope. The implication: your despair suggests you’re not.
"Who, being innocent, has ever perished? Those who plow evil and sow trouble reap it." The retribution principle stated clearly. It will be dismantled across the next 38 chapters.
Even lions perish when God withdraws. Eliphaz uses creation imagery: if God’s judgment reaches the strongest animals, no human is exempt.
Calvin: Eliphaz’s principle is partly true — God does judge the wicked. His error is applying it mechanically to every individual case.
Calvin: sowing and reaping is biblical (Gal 6:7). But Eliphaz turns a general truth into an absolute law, and absolutes break on exceptions.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes nāqî ("innocent/clean") is a legal term — acquitted, found not guilty. Eliphaz is saying: no one acquitted by God has ever been destroyed.
The NET connects "plow evil, sow trouble, reap it" to the agricultural metaphor tradition in Hosea 10:13 and Galatians 6:7–8.
Clines: Eliphaz’s question is not rhetorical for him — he genuinely believes the innocent are always vindicated. The book of Job exists to show this is not always true in the present age.
Clines: "those who plow evil reap it" is Eliphaz’s Proverbs theology. It works as a general principle. It fails as a diagnostic tool.
Habel: Eliphaz is the first expert witness. His testimony: the retribution principle is universally valid. Job’s suffering proves hidden guilt.

sections.header: Verses 12–21 — The Night Vision: "Can a Mortal Be Righteous Before God?"

heb.paragraph: The night vision comes in silence (dəmāmâ) and with a spirit (rûaḥ) passing before Eliphaz’s face. The hair of his flesh stands up. This is the only reported supernatural experience among the friends. Its message: no mortal can be righteous before God.

"Can a mortal (ʾĕnōsh) be more righteous than God?" The word ʾĕnōsh emphasises human frailty and mortality. The vision’s point: if God does not trust his own angels, how much less can he trust mortals, who are crushed like moths?

panels.ctx: Eliphaz reports a nocturnal vision — a whispered voice in the night. Its theology: no creature can be righteous before its Creator. Even angels are not trusted. Humans, who live in houses of clay, are crushed without anyone noticing. The vision is authentic but its application is misguided: Eliphaz uses it to argue that Job’s suffering is inevitable, not unjust.

cross.note: "A still small voice" — God’s communication in whispers, not storms (until ch 38).
"No one living is righteous before you" — the same theology, affirmed by the Psalmist.

notes.note: The night vision: a form, a face, a whisper. Eliphaz’s mystical experience lends authority to his speech. This is not philosophical argument but claimed revelation.
"Can a mortal be more righteous than God?" The question is correct in the abstract. Its misapplication: using divine transcendence to dismiss Job’s complaint.

"If God places no trust in his servants, if he charges his angels with error — how much more those who live in houses of clay?" The argument from greater to lesser: if angels fall short, humans are dust.
Calvin: the principle is orthodox — no creature is righteous compared to the Creator. But Eliphaz wields it as a weapon: if you suffer, you must have sinned, because all humans sin.
Calvin: "houses of clay" — the fragility of the body. The image is true but the inference is wrong: fragility does not prove guilt.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET describes the vision with clinical precision: "a word was secretly brought to me, my ears caught a whisper." The vocabulary of prophetic reception.
The NET discusses the translation options: "more righteous than" (comparative) vs "righteous before" (absolute). Both are grammatically possible; the second fits the context better.
Clines: the night vision is genuine but its conclusion is a non sequitur. "No mortal is righteous before God" does not answer "why is this specific innocent man suffering?"
Clines: the question could also be translated "Can a mortal be righteous before God?" — not comparative but existential. Eliphaz is saying: human righteousness is inherently inadequate.
Habel: Eliphaz introduces supernatural testimony — a night vision that supports the prosecution’s case. The message: no human can claim innocence before the divine court.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV“Consider now: Who, being innocent, has ever perished? Where were the upright ever destroyed?ESV“Remember: who that was innocent ever perished? Or where were the upright cut off?NIVThen Eliphaz the Temanite replied:ESVThen Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said:

rec.text: "Eliphaz opens with respect. He acknowledges Job’s past ministry to others. This is not a cruel man; it is a sincere one with a flawed framework."

rows.label: Gentle Opening — 4:1–6
rows.text: "Your piety should be your confidence." Encouragement hiding accusation.

rows.label: The Vision — 4:12–21
rows.text: A spirit in the night: "Can a mortal be righteous before God?"

hebtext.note: "Who, being innocent (nāqî), has ever perished?" (v.7). Eliphaz states the retribution principle: the innocent do not suffer ultimately. This is not stupid theology — it is the mainstream position of Proverbs and Deuteronomy. Eliphaz’s error is not that the principle is always wrong but that it is always right.

The night vision comes in silence (dəmāmâ) and with a spirit (rûaḥ) passing before Eliphaz’s face. The hair of his flesh stands up. This is the only reported supernatural experience among the friends. Its message: no mortal can be righteous before God.

"Can a mortal (ʾĕnōsh) be more righteous than God?" The word ʾĕnōsh emphasises human frailty and mortality. The vision’s point: if God does not trust his own angels, how much less can he trust mortals, who are crushed like moths?

thread.text: "The LORD does not let the righteous go hungry" — the proverbial basis for Eliphaz’s theology.
"I have never seen the righteous forsaken" — the experiential claim Eliphaz makes.
"A still small voice" — God’s communication in whispers, not storms (until ch 38).
"No one living is righteous before you" — the same theology, affirmed by the Psalmist.

themes.note: Justice peaks: the retribution principle is Eliphaz’s thesis. Judgment is high — the night vision asserts no mortal can be clean. Prophecy: the vision claims supernatural authority.
